questions.q: What does PCA do?
options: Squeezes many columns into a few while keeping most of the information
questions.explain: PCA reduces dimensions: many columns → a few, keeping most variance (information).

questions.q: PC1 (the first principal component) is:
options: The direction of MAXIMUM variance (most spread)
questions.explain: PC1 points where the data spreads out the most — it holds the most information.

questions.q: Are PCA's new components your original columns?
options: No — they are NEW columns made by mixing the originals
Yes, it keeps some columns and drops others
questions.explain: PCA is feature EXTRACTION: it builds new blended components, not a subset of original columns.

questions.q: Why must you SCALE before PCA?
options: PCA chases variance, so big-range columns would dominate
questions.explain: Unscaled large-range features look like they have all the variance, so PCA follows only them.

questions.q: `PCA(n_components=0.95)` means:
options: Keep enough components to retain 95% of the variance
questions.explain: A value between 0 and 1 = the fraction of information to keep; PCA picks how many components that needs.

questions.q: In PCA, 'variance' roughly means:
options: How spread out / different the data points are
questions.explain: High variance = the points differ a lot in that direction = useful information.

questions.q: Does PCA use the label (y)?
options: No — it only looks at the features (X)
questions.explain: PCA is unsupervised: it works on the features only and ignores the label.

questions.q: A main reason to use PCA is:
options: To increase the number of columns
To see high-dimensional data in 2D and/or speed up models
questions.explain: Fewer, cleaner columns → you can plot the data and models run faster with less noise.

questions.q: t-SNE and UMAP are mainly used for:
questions.explain: They squeeze many columns into 2 so you can SEE if clusters exist.

questions.q: Should you feed t-SNE output into a model?
options: No — it's for looking only; its distances are distorted
questions.explain: t-SNE distorts distances (like flattening a crumpled map) — use it for the picture, not for modelling.

questions.q: t-SNE mainly preserves:
options: Local structure — nearby points stay nearby
questions.explain: t-SNE keeps local neighbourhoods: points close in high-D stay close in 2D.

questions.q: If your data has only 2 columns, do you need t-SNE?
options: No — just plot the 2 columns directly
questions.explain: t-SNE is for MANY columns. With 2, plot them straight with matplotlib.

questions.q: UMAP compared to t-SNE is usually:
options: Faster (and often keeps the big-picture layout better)
questions.explain: UMAP is generally faster than t-SNE and often preserves global structure better.

questions.q: How do t-SNE and matplotlib relate?
options: t-SNE shrinks many columns to 2, THEN matplotlib draws them
questions.explain: t-SNE makes many-column data drawable; matplotlib draws the result. They work together.

questions.q: You have 50 columns and want to SEE the data. Best move?
options: Use t-SNE/UMAP to get 2 columns, then plot
questions.explain: Plotting 2 raw columns hides the rest; t-SNE/UMAP uses all 50 to arrange points, then you plot.

questions.q: The distances between clusters in a t-SNE plot are:
questions.explain: The squeezing distorts distances, so gaps between clusters are not trustworthy.

questions.q: An anomaly is:
options: A rare point that is different from 'normal'
questions.explain: Anomaly = the odd one out: rare and unlike the normal data (fraud, failures, attacks).

questions.q: Why does a normal classifier struggle with fraud?
options: Extreme imbalance, very few labels, and brand-new patterns
questions.explain: Fraud is <1%, has few labels, and new tricks have no past examples — so supervised struggles.

questions.q: The main idea of unsupervised anomaly detection is:
options: Learn what NORMAL looks like, then flag what doesn't fit
questions.explain: We have lots of normal data → learn normal, flag anything different.

questions.q: Isolation Forest flags anomalies by:
options: How FEW random cuts are needed to isolate a point
questions.explain: Odd points are far from the crowd, so very few random cuts isolate them. Few cuts = anomaly.

questions.q: In Isolation Forest, a label of -1 means:
questions.explain: -1 = anomaly/outlier, 1 = normal (inlier).

questions.q: `contamination=0.01` means:
options: You expect about 1% of points to be anomalies
questions.explain: contamination sets where the cut line goes — here it flags the weirdest ~1%.

questions.q: A model that always predicts 'normal' on data with 0.1% fraud is:
options: ~99.9% accurate but useless — it catches zero fraud
questions.explain: Accuracy lies on rare events: high accuracy, but it never catches fraud.

questions.q: LOF (Local Outlier Factor) detects an anomaly by:
options: Checking if a point is lonely while its neighbours are crowded
questions.explain: LOF compares a point's local density to its neighbours' — lonely-among-crowds = outlier.

questions.q: What does one neuron do?
options: Multiply inputs by weights, add them up, then apply an activation
questions.explain: A neuron: multiply × weights → add (z) → squish/activate → output.

questions.q: During training, what does a neural network actually learn?
questions.explain: Training adjusts the weights so the guesses get better.

questions.q: Deep learning is:
options: A neural network with MANY (hidden) layers
questions.explain: Deep learning = neural networks that are 'deep' — many layers stacked.

questions.q: A neural network is part of:
questions.explain: A neural network is one type of ML model — same family as trees and K-Means.

questions.q: What is an activation function for?
options: To add non-linearity so the network can learn complex patterns
questions.explain: Without activation, the whole network is just plain arithmetic; activations let it learn complex things.

questions.q: A typical neural network is:
questions.explain: Most NNs (e.g. image classifiers) are supervised — they learn from labeled answers.

questions.q: The sentence vector [1,1,0,1,0] means:
options: Which words from the list ARE present (1) or not (0)
questions.explain: Bag-of-words: 1 = the word is in the sentence, 0 = it is not.

questions.q: Adding more layers generally lets a network:
options: Learn more complex patterns (but it needs more data)
questions.explain: More layers = more capacity for complex patterns, at the cost of needing more data/compute.

questions.q: The loss (the 'oops score') measures:
options: How WRONG the model's guesses are
questions.explain: Loss is one number for how wrong the model is now; training tries to shrink it.

questions.q: Gradient descent does:
options: Steps the weights DOWNHILL to reduce the loss
questions.explain: It repeatedly nudges weights toward lower loss — walking downhill on the loss 'mountain'.

questions.q: Backpropagation does:
options: Figures out how much each weight is to blame (computes the gradients)
questions.explain: Backprop goes backward through the network to find each weight's share of the error.

questions.q: The learning rate is:
options: The STEP SIZE for each weight update
questions.explain: Learning rate = how big a step you take downhill each update.

questions.q: If the learning rate is too BIG:
options: The loss overshoots and bounces / diverges
questions.explain: Too-big steps overshoot the valley and can make the loss get worse and blow up.

questions.q: Which optimizer is the common default to start with?
questions.explain: Adam (momentum + adaptive steps) is the usual go-to default optimizer.

questions.q: The sigmoid function squashes a number into the range:
questions.explain: Sigmoid squashes any input into 0–1 — handy for a probability/'yes meter'.

questions.q: ReLU does what to a NEGATIVE number?
questions.explain: ReLU = max(0, x): negatives become 0, positives stay. (Leaky ReLU lets a tiny bit through.)
